summary: Gruber dismantles a Pando article by Mark Ames that attempted to draw an equivalence between Apple's policy of not hiring convicted felons for campus construction and Indiana's anti-gay discrimination law. He catches Ames citing misleading statistics about criminal records that conflate arrests and misdemeanors with felonies, inflating the affected population from roughly 1-in-8 working-age men to 30 percent of all Americans. Gruber also corrects Ames's claim that Apple 'authorized' or 'released' the book Becoming Steve Jobs, noting that executives merely granted interviews. He further undercuts Ames's suggestion that the book whitewashes Jobs's history by showing that it directly covers the damning no-poaching emails Ames himself cited. The piece is a methodical takedown of sloppy, agenda-driven journalism that relies on false equivalences and misrepresented facts.
key_points: Ames's comparison of Apple's no-felons construction policy to Indiana's anti-gay discrimination law is a false equivalence
Ames cited a 30 percent criminal record statistic that includes arrests and misdemeanors, not just felonies — the actual ex-felon rate among working-age men is about 1-in-8
Apple did not authorize or release Becoming Steve Jobs; executives simply granted interviews with no editorial control
The book Ames claims is a 'rewrite' of Apple's history actually covers the same damning Jobs emails Ames highlighted, undermining his whitewashing claim
The piece illustrates how Pando's editorial approach distorts facts to fit a predetermined anti-Apple narrative
money_quotes: I feel safe saying that not hiring felons for construction jobs is not even in the same ballpark as a statewide law allowing for discrimination against people based on their sexuality.
Ames either doesn't know what a felony is, or he does know and is deliberately misleading Pando readers by citing an unrelated statistic.
It's pure bullshit to imply that 30 percent of Americans are banned from construction jobs at Apple's new campus.
1-in-8 working age men is an eye-opening number to be sure, and construction workers are overwhelmingly male, but that's not even close to Ames's '30 percent' figure.
Apple did not 'authorize' Becoming Steve Jobs. Apple did not 'release' it.
key_insight: Gruber systematically exposes how agenda-driven journalism uses misleading statistics and false equivalences to manufacture outrage against its chosen targets.
